coredata.dc:title: Influence of precursor size in the hydrothermal synthesis of cellulose-based carbon nanodots and its application towards solar cell sensitization
coredata.dc:creator: Sta. Ana, Jomar C.
Camacho, Drexel H.
coredata.dc:description: The utilization of the abundant yet environmentally polluting green algae as renewable source for new materials is highly desirable. This study presents the utilization of raw cellulose (RC) and cellulose nanocrystals (CNC) extracted from the green algae Cladophora rupestris as precursors for carbon nanodots (CNDs) and its potential use as photosensitizer in solar cell. The results of the hydrothermal treatment of the celluloses afforded spherical CNDs with average dimension of 5.79 ± 1.60 nm (TEM) and having crystalline property (d-spacing of 0.253 ± 0.050 nm) indicating graphitic nature. The size distribution and the ease of the CND formation have been shown to be influenced by the size and morphology of the cellulose precursor. The nanometer-sized crystalline CNCs afforded higher CND yield (54.6 ± 2.71%) compared to the micrometer-sized amorphous RC (37.2 ± 2.76% CND yield). However the size polydispersity of the latter was observed to be narrower at 10.0 ± 3.74 nm (AFM) compared to the former (76.9 ± 73.1 nm; AFM) attributed to clustering or aggregation aided by the presence of hydrophilic functional groups. Indeed, the FT-IR and TOF-SIMs analyses of the CNDs confirmed the presence of hydroxyls, oxides, and carbonyl functional groups that can facilitate the clustering. The CNDs exhibit photoluminescence upon UV irradiation and optical characterization using UV–Vis and Fluorescence Spectroscopy supported the structural and functional group analysis of carbon nanodots. Fabrication of the CND-sensitized solar cell showed the potential of CNDs as photosensitizer albeit in lower yield compared to the positive control.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Photosensitizer
Dye-sensitized solar cell
Cladophora
Cellulose
Cellulose nanocrystal

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of carbon nanodots-sensitized solar cell
Electrochemical characterization of carbon nanodots sensitized solar cell
Electrochemical characterization of carbon nanodots sensitized solar cell (CND-SSC)
Electrochemical impedance measurement of carbon nanodots sensitized solar cell

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) TEM image of CNDs from Cladophora Cellulose, B) size distribution of CNDs from Cladophora Cellulose. C) CNDs from Cladophora Cellulose viewed at lower scaling. D) Magnified image of single CNDs showing ordered crystal structure.
AFM images and its corresponding size distribution for different polysaccharide precursor: A) Cladophora Raw Cellulose (RC) CNDs, B) Cladophora Cellulose nanocrystal (CNC) CNDs.
FT-IR spectra of cellulose-based carbon nanodots as indicated, compared with the initial precursor FT-IR spectra.
TOF-SIMs negative ion mass spectra of cellulose CNDs: A) Cladophora Raw cellulose CNDs and B) Cladophora Cellulose nanocrystal (CNC) CNDs. Inset: Ion Fragments and Structural Assignments.
UV–Vis spectra of carbon nanodots (CND) from a) Cladophora Raw Cellulose (RC), and b) Cladophora cellulose nanocrystal (CNC).

Fluorescence emission and its deconvolution spectra of CNDs from (A) Cladophora Raw Cellulose (RC), and (B) Cladophora cellulose nanocrystal (CNC). [Insets: CND solutions under daylight (left) and under UV light (right)]. Condition: slit width: 10 nm; wavelength scan: 800-200 nm; excitation wavelength: 365 nm.
Light and dark current-voltage curves of Cellulose-based CNDs-sensitized solar cells (A) CNDs from Cladophora raw cellulose (RC); and (B) CNDs from Cladophora cellulose nanocrsytal (CNC).
Nyquist plot of polysaccharide CNDs-sensitized solar cell from Cladophora raw cellulose (RC), and CNC Inset: Equivalent circuit model; EIS analysis of Carbon nanodots sensitized solar cells.
Solar-cell parameters for CNDS-sensitized solar cells derived from IV characteristics. a

Where Voc is the open-circuit voltage; Isc is the short-circuit current; FF is the fill factor and η is the efficiency.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Negative control is the DSSC configuration without dye, or CND.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Positive control is the DSSC using Ruthenium dye [28].

head.ce:title: Influence of precursor size in the hydrothermal synthesis of cellulose-based carbon nanodots and its application towards solar cell sensitization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, De La Salle University, 2401 Taft Avenue, Manila 0922, Philippines

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Organic Materials and Interfaces Unit, CENSER, De La Salle University, 2401 Taft Avenue, Manila 0922, Philippines

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Chemistry Department, De La Salle University, 2401 Taft Avenue, Manila 0922, Philippines.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The utilization of the abundant yet environmentally polluting green algae as renewable source for new materials is highly desirable. This study presents the utilization of raw cellulose (RC) and cellulose nanocrystals (CNC) extracted from the green algae Cladophora rupestris as precursors for carbon nanodots (CNDs) and its potential use as photosensitizer in solar cell. The results of the hydrothermal treatment of the celluloses afforded spherical CNDs with average dimension of 5.79 ± 1.60 nm (TEM) and having crystalline property (d-spacing of 0.253 ± 0.050 nm) indicating graphitic nature. The size distribution and the ease of the CND formation have been shown to be influenced by the size and morphology of the cellulose precursor. The nanometer-sized crystalline CNCs afforded higher CND yield (54.6 ± 2.71%) compared to the micrometer-sized amorphous RC (37.2 ± 2.76% CND yield). However the size polydispersity of the latter was observed to be narrower at 10.0 ± 3.74 nm (AFM) compared to the former (76.9 ± 73.1 nm; AFM) attributed to clustering or aggregation aided by the presence of hydrophilic functional groups. Indeed, the FT-IR and TOF-SIMs analyses of the CNDs confirmed the presence of hydroxyls, oxides, and carbonyl functional groups that can facilitate the clustering. The CNDs exhibit photoluminescence upon UV irradiation and optical characterization using UV–Vis and Fluorescence Spectroscopy supported the structural and functional group analysis of carbon nanodots. Fabrication of the CND-sensitized solar cell showed the potential of CNDs as photosensitizer albeit in lower yield compared to the positive control.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (CNDs) are nanomaterials within the size range 1–10 nm mostly composed of carbon [1]. They have emerged as a promising material due to their benign and inexpensive nature [2]. They also exhibit the desirable water dispersibility and photoluminescence [3,4]. Since its serendipitous discovery in 2004, much progress has been achieved in the synthesis, properties, and application of carbon nanodots [2,4–6]. CND applications have been reported in the field of biomedicine, catalysis, and chemical sensor, among others [5–8]. In the field of optoelectronics, CNDs were used in photovoltaic devices [9], and as sensitizer in solar cells [10]. In the synthesis of CNDs, the hydrothermal carbonization technique (HCT) has achieved significant attention owing to its simple set-up and environmental approach in converting biomass into higher value carbonaceous materials [11]. A survey of literature revealed that carbon nanodots could be prepared from carbon-rich biomass such as polysaccharides upon hydrothermal treatment [6,12]. However, investigations on the influence of the precursor size towards CND synthesis have not been explored. We report herein for the first time the effect of precursor size in the preparation of CNDs.

An interesting biomass is the Cladophora green algae that bloom out of proportion as triggered by changes in water chemistry causing the algal bloom problem. The uncontrolled blooming of algae leads to ecological imbalance, fish kills, and major problems to local folks. The utilization of these abundant, yet unwanted biomass is highly sought [13]. In the last decade, it has been shown that the cellulose extracted from Cladophora algae posses interesting properties compared to wood cellulose [14]. It has higher degree of crystallinity, larger surface area (63–95 m2/g vs. 1 m2/g via N2 gas adsorption), and higher pore volume (0.554 cm3/g vs. 0.003 cm3/g; BET N2 adsorption) compared to cellulose derived from plants [15,16]. Recently, this highly crystalline Cladophora cellulose has been applied as components in filter media, drug carriers, rheology enhancers and conductive cellulose composites [17]. In our previous works, we reported distinguishable characteristics for Cladophora cellulose obtained from a volcanic freshwater lake compared to a marine algae [18] and its utilization as reinforcement fibers [19] and as paper-based electrode for heavy metal detection [20]. We report herein the utilization of these abundant yet environmentally polluting algae for the synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanodots and its applications towards solar cell sensitization.

Solar cells are promising device for power conversion [21]. However, it remains a challenge to achieve an efficient, mass reproducible, and inexpensive device [22]. The third generation photovoltaic, especially the Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells (DSSCs), has a strong potential owing to its cheaper cost and ease of fabrication [23]. The typical configuration of DSSC involves mesoporous TiO2 layer sintered on transparent conducting glass. A dye, which acts as charge transfer photosensitizer, is attached to the mesoporous layer. A redox electrolyte (I3/I− redox couple) and counter electrode (Pt-coated FTO glass) completes the system [24,25]. Among the DSSC components, the sensitizer is the heart of the device since they trigger and maintain the electron transfer processes in a photo-electrochemical system by absorbing solar radiation [26]. Replacement of the usual ruthenium complex dye sensitizer is highly desirable towards cheap, stable, environmentally benign, and efficient solar cell. Recent technologies involve semiconductor quantum dots (Cd and Pb) but its toxicity is problem. A more environment-friendly sensitizer is highly sought for solar cell application, hence this study.

The raw cellulose (RC) and cellulose nanocrystal (CNC) were extracted from the environmentally polluting green algae Cladophora rupestris, harvested from Taal Lake, Talisay, Batangas, Philippines using the method of Sucaldito and Camacho [19]. It was chosen because of its abundance and yearlong availability in Taal Lake. Moreover, it offers unique cellulosic materials that are different from plant-based cellulose. Solar cells were fabricated using TiO2 nano-powder (anatase 99%; 10–25 nm; US Research nanomaterials, Inc.), Triton-X (Sigma Aldrich) and Acetylacetone (Sigma Aldrich), FTO conducting glass (Sigma Aldrich), electrolyte solution (Iodolyte AN-50; Solaronix), counter-electrode Pt catalyst (Platisol; Solaronix), liquid detergent solution (RBS™ 25 solution; Sigma Aldrich) Ethanol (Merck, Inc.), and TiCl4 (99.9%; Acros Organics).

Carbon nanodots (CNDs) were prepared using the method by Wu et al. [11], with slight modification. Two grams (2.0 g) of the cellulose extracted from Cladophora rupestris were dispersed in 40 mL distilled water and were agitated by ultrasonication for 15 min. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave (100 mL). The sealed autoclave was placed in an oven at 220 °C for 6 h. Following cooling, the mixture was subjected to centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 20 min to separate the black solid particle (hydrochar) from the mixture. The yellowish-brown mixture obtained was collected and then dialyzed using a dialysis tube (1000 Da molecular weight cut off) for 24 h and further purified using 0.2 μm PVDF syringe filter (Whatman) to afford a carbon nanodots (CNDs) from the Cladophora raw cellulose (RC). The same process was used to prepare carbon nanodots from Cladophora cellulose nanocrystals (CNC).

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM): The size and size distribution were conducted using AFM (XE – 100, Park Systems) and TEM (JEM 2100F, JEOL). The carbon nanodots solution was dropped-casted on mica and dried inside a desiccator before AFM analysis while carbon nanodots were deposited on copper grids coated with lacey carbon prior to analysis by TEM characterization. Size and d-spacing measurements were done using Image J software [27].

FT-IR analysis of CNDs: The FTIR spectra were obtained using an FTIR spectrophotometer (Nexus 6700, NICOLET) using KBr pellet method. The changes in functional groups were monitored in the course of preparation of the CNDs. Spectra in the range of 4000–400 cm−1 were obtained in a resolution of 4 cm−1 by accumulating 64 scans at room temperature.

Time-of-Flight Secondary Mass spectrometry: The work was carried using TOF-SIMS V (IONTOF) in static mode. Samples were analyzed at a pressure of approximately 1.6 × 10−9 mbar and bombarded with Bi3++ primary ions at an energy of 30 keV. SIMS operates with a 1.5812 pA beam current rastered over a frame area 500 μm × 500 μm with a pixel resolution of 128 × 128 pixel. The gun was operated with 11.2 ns pulse width, for an ion dose of 1.3 × 1011, lower than the threshold level of 1 × 1013 ions cm−2 for static conditions. Effective charge compensation was obtained by using the electron flood gun. Secondary ions were detected with a Reflectron time-of-flight analyzer, a multichannel plate (MCPs), and a time-to-digital converter (TDC). Measurements were performed with an acquisition time of 100 s, at a TDC time resolution of 50 ps. A spectral analysis using the provided software was performed to identify the characteristic ions associated with peaks in the mass spectrum. Peak intensities were measured as the corrected area of the peak, normalized by the total ion intensity in the spectrum.

UV–vis spectroscopy: Absorption spectra of carbon nanodots solution were recorded using UV/Vis spectrometer (HITACHI Y-2009) and were carried out in deionized water and monitored using wavelength scan in the range from 400 to 190 nm.

Fluorescence spectroscopy: Fluorescence spectra were recorded using Fluorescence spectrophotometer (PERKIN ELMER LS 4) and carried out at normal temperature and pressure in deionized water. Experimental conditions: slit width: 10 nm; wavelength scan: 800-200 nm; excitation wavelength: 365 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of carbon nanodots-sensitized solar cell
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication of CND-sensitized solar cell was done following the work of Bantang and Camacho [28] with slight modification. A pair of conducting glass (FTO) with a size of 2 × 2 cm2 was prepared and cleaned with liquid detergent solution (RBS™ 25 solution) and then subjected to sonication for 15 min, washed with deionized water and then with ethanol. The cleaned conducting glass (FTO) was immersed on the aqueous solution of 40 mM TiCl4 in a beaker at 70 °C for 20 min and dried. The fabrication of the photoanode was done by placing Scoth® Magic™ tape (∼50 μm thicknesses) on the parallel sides of an FTO conducting glass that formed a 1 × 1 cm2 active area. For the preparation of the titanium dioxide paste, a given amount of titanium dioxide (2.5 g) nanoparticles was dispersed by ultrasonication and then stirred in a solvent mixture that composed of the following: 20 mL absolute ethanol, 0.60 mL Triton X-100 and 0.80 mL acetylacetone adapted from the work of Xu et al., 2011 [1]. Titanium dioxide paste was coated on the conducting surface of the FTO glass using doctor blade method and was sintered at 450 °C in a muffle furnace for 30 min. Upon cooling to 80 °C, it was soaked in an aqueous solution of TiCl4, washed with water, then ethanol and dried. Lastly, the Titania-coated FTO conducting glass was immersed in a one-day old previously prepared aqueous solution of carbon nanodots. The counter-electrode was prepared by brushing the FTO conducting glass with an alcoholic solution of platinum (Platisol, Solaronix) and then sintered at 450 °C in a muffle furnace for 30 min. The CND-sensitized solar cell (open configuration) was prepared by sandwiching the electrolyte solution (Iodolyte AN 50, Solaronix) in between the photoanode and the counter electrode. The cell was secured by attaching two clips at each side of the cell. The same process was used for the fabrication of the positive control DSSC using Ru dye (Ruthenizer 535-bisTBA, Solaronix) and the negative control DSSC without CND or dye.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of carbon nanodots sensitized solar cell
ce:section.ce:para: The determination of solar cell parameter such as open circuit voltage (Voc), short-circuit current (Isc), fill factor (FF) and efficiency (ŋ) were determined by two-electrode set-up linear sweep voltammetry using the following conditions: start potential at −0.800 V, stop potential at 0.00V and scan rate at 10 mV 100 mW/cm2. The AM 1.5 solar simulator was used as the light source. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was employed to determine the electron mobility in the device. In brief, the impedance of the device was measured using the following parameters under illumination (100 mW, AM 1.5 solar cell): the applied amplitude of 10 mV, applied voltage equivalent to the open circuit voltage, and at the frequency range between 0.01 to 1 × 106 Hz.

Two forms of cellulose precursors were prepared from the green algae Cladophora rupestris. One form is the raw cellulose (RC) extracted from C. rupestris, while the other form is the cellulose nanocrystals (CNC) obtained by HBr hydrolysis of RC. The two precursors are both celluloses but differ in terms of their morphology and fiber dimensions. The RC has a compact aggregated amorphous fiber in micro dimensions (diameter 0.53 ± 0.09 μm) while the CNC showed loose crystalline filaments having a width in nanoscale (diameter 20.0 ± 4.4 nm) and with higher degree of crystallinity [19]. These cellulose precursors form white solution when dispersed in water, but upon hydrothermal carbonization (HTC), the solution changed to yellowish-brown accompanied by a noticeable formation of black aggregates. The change in solution color qualitatively signifies successful conversion of natural polysaccharide precursors into CNDs (dispersed in the supernatant) and hydrochar (black aggregated particles). The formation of colored solution is typical for hydrothermal formation of CNDs. The formation of CNDs from chitin and l-arginine precursors has been shown to form yellow solution [29] whereas; CNDs from glucose, chitosan and l-cysteine afforded brown CNDs solution [22]. Separation of the supernatant and hydrochar components through high-speed centrifugation, dialysis and syringe filtration afforded the CNDs. Yield is a major factor that should be considered in terms of CND synthesis to make it more practical [30]. Results show that the initial form of the precursor affects the ease of CND formation. The CND yield is higher (54.6 ± 2.71%) for the CNC precursor compared to the RC precursor (37.2 ± 2.76%). The difference in yield is attributed to the morphological differences of the precursors. The loose nanofilament structures of the CNC precursor has greater propensity towards conversion to carbon nanodots as compared to the more compact, aggregated, and amorphous micro-fibers of the RC precursor. The current accepted mechanism of CND formation from polysaccharide precursor is the fragmentation of the polymer into smaller chains leading to the formation of sugar monomers [31]. Thus the smaller the fiber dimension, the more exposed the cellulose chain and the easier for the fragmentation to occur. The loosening of the CNC nanofibers is attributed to the hydrolysis treatment in the preparation of the CNC as the amorphous region is removed leaving the nanocrystalline component [19].

The CNDs produced from Cladophora rupestris showed nanoparticles with spherical shape. TEM analysis for CNDs from RC precursor (Fig. 1 ) showed a well dispersed spherically shaped carbon nanodots with average mean diameter of 5.79 ± 1.60 nm (n = 100). As compared to the current work, the CNDs from hydrothermal carbonization of cellulose (Avicel PH-101) precursor [32] and CNDs from carboxymethyl cellulose [11] have larger average sizes of 100 and 30.2 nm, respectively. The size differences can be attributed to post-synthesis purification method. Yan et al. [32], simply applied high-speed centrifugation while Wu et al. [11], further used dialysis after centrifugation lowering the average size and polydispersity of the CNDs. The utilization of syringe filtration after centrifugation and dialysis in this current work ensured elimination of unconverted precursors in the final CNDs solution. High-resolution TEM also revealed the crystalline structure of the CNDs with d-spacing measurement of 0.253 (±0.050) nm (n = 30 lattice plane readings) (Fig. 1-D). Although there is a possibility of CNDs adapting other crystalline structures depending on the water content of prepared CNDs, the observed d-spacing is in the range 0.21–0.25 nm characteristic of the (100) facet of graphite at high water content [2]. The same (100) facet of graphite at water content below 10% is expected to form larger d-spacing at ∼0.33 nm [11]. Unfortunately, TEM image of CND from CNC precursor is hard to obtain hence AFM data was used for comparison.

AFM analysis confirmed the formation of a spherical nanoparticle CNDs produced from Cladophora rupestris. However, due to AFM tip convolution effect the diameter estimation of CNDs using AFM resulted to the overestimation of lateral dimensions compared to TEM observations [10]. The results show that the form of the initial precursor affects the CND size. The RC precursor afforded smaller CND nanoparticles (10.0 ± 3.74 nm (n = 50)) compared to the CNC precursor (76.9 ± 73.1 nm (n = 100)) (Fig. 2 ). These particle dimensions are relatively larger than the CNDs from sucrose (blue luminescent CNDs: 5 nm and green luminescent CNDs: 2 nm) [33]. The size of CND has been shown to be dependent on the precursor size. The small molecule precursor such as sucrose gave smaller CND while the polymeric cellulose generally afforded larger CND size. This was further supported by Briscoe et al. [29], who reported larger CNDs from chitosan and chitin precursors as compared to the small molecule glucose precursor indicating the role of molecule size as vital in the ease of CND formation and eventually its size. The nitrogen-containing carbohydrates formed larger CNDs because the proposed mechanism of CND involves Mailard reaction between resulting nitrogen-containing compounds and hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) as compared to glucose that involves only the intermediate HMF in the hydrothermal carbonization [31,34]. In the current work, the CNC precursors have smaller size compared to the RC precursor but the CNDs produced from CNC were found to have larger size than those produced from the RC precursor. We surmise this as attributed to the concentration effect of the as-produced CND. Since the CND from CNC precursor afforded twice the CND yield compared to the RC precursor, the tendency for aggregation most likely occurs on more concentrated CND especially if the CNDs contain surface chemistry ideal for aggregation. It has been accounted that carbon nanodots containing hydrophilic surface functionalities such as carbonyl and hydroxides are prone to form aggregates or cluster together due to hydrogen bonding [22]. FT-IR and TOF-SIMs data (see section 3.3) showed the hydrophilic functionalities such as the carbonyls, oxides, and hydroxyl functional groups indicating the propensity for the CNDs towards aggregation/clustering. This could account for the observed larger CNDs from CNC precursor. Thus the nanometer-sized crystalline CNCs form higher yield and aggregated CNDs compared to the micrometer-sized amorphous raw cellulose (RC). This demonstrates the influence of precursor size in the hydrothermal synthesis of carbon nanodots.

Surface functionalities on CNDs are important factor affecting its properties and eventually its function. FT-IR analysis (Fig. 3 ) revealed that most functionalities from the precursor are retained on the carbon nanodots such as the hydroxide stretching (3410 cm−1), CH stretching (2920 cm−1), OH stretching (1627 cm−1), CO stretching (1220 and 1160 cm−1), and CH rocking vibration of carbohydrates (985 cm−1) [19]. This suggests that the carbon nanodots contain hydrophilic functionalities, which also accounts for the good water solubility of CNDs. This is in good agreement with Yan et al. [32], where hydrothermal carbonization method was also used to produce carbon nanodots from cellulose and also identified the presence of both carbonyl and hydroxide functional groups using FT-IR analysis. An obvious functional group observed only after hydrothermal carbonization but is absent from the precursors is the peak at 1710 cm−1 attributed to carbonyl functionality, CO. This is suggestive of the formation of hydroxymethylfurfural, which is the known intermediate in the CND formation from glucose.

Further analysis on the CND surface functionality using negative ion TOF-SIMs spectroscopic analysis (Fig. 4 ) show the anion surface composition revealing the presence of hydrophilic functional groups in the form of carbonyls, hydroxides and carbon-based oxides. This demonstrates for the first time the use of TOF-SIMS technique in the hydrophilic functional group identification on the CND surface. The data confirms the FT-IR results on the presence of hydrophilic functional group. The type of surface functionality present on the as-produced carbon nanodots from the cellulose precursor is essential since it will enable the effective binding/immobilization of the photosensitizer (Carbon nanodots) into the semiconductor metal oxide nanoparticles (TiO2), thus allowing initiation of electron injection from the photosensitizer into the conduction band of the metal oxide [35].

The UV–vis absorption of the CNDs scanned from 190 to 400 nm (Fig. 5 ) shows absorbance peak around 250–300 nm. CNDs obtained from cellulose-based precursors also recorded absorption peak centered at 250 nm-300 nm [11,32]. These absorptions are attributed to π-π* electron transition on the graphitic surface due to π conjugated aromatic system and n-π* transition of the carbonyl and other oxygen-containing compounds [2,11,36,37]. Comparison of lambda max showed a slight red shift from 282 nm to 285 nm for RC CNDs and CNC CNDs, respectively. This change is attributed either to the formation of more conjugated system or a more graphitic CND structure [2].

The solutions of the as-synthesized CNDs under daylight (Fig. 6 inset) showed pale yellow to yellowish-brown color but showed green luminescence upon UV illumination. Wavelength scanning in fluorescence emission spectroscopy on aqueous CNDs solution from different cellulose precursors (Fig. 6) showed three distinct regions I, II and III. Deconvolution of the emission spectra revealed 2 distinct peaks in region II. The multi-emission behavior of the CNDs can be attributed to differences in size distribution or surface functionalities of the CNDs [38,39]. The size distribution effect may dominate because of the apparent polydispersity of the CNDs as revealed by AFM image (Fig. 2) [40]. The differences on surface functionalities may also trigger multi emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of carbon nanodots sensitized solar cell (CND-SSC)
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of the prepared carbon nanodots as sensitizer in a solar cell (CND-SSC) replacing the conventional dye, was determined using standard AM 1.5 simulated sunlight (100 mW/cm2). The current-voltage characteristics were measured under dark and illuminated conditions (Fig. 7 ). During the characterization at dark condition, the solar cell configuration showed a diode property [41]. Almost no current is generated under dark condition however, upon light illumination, the current-voltage curve shifted upward and the solar cell generates current [42].

The sensitizer (CNDs) adsorbed light and trigger change of state from ground state (S) to excited state (S∗). The excited state (S*) is oxidized to S+ then an electron is injected into the conduction band of the semiconductor (TiO2). The electron moves toward the surface of the conducting glass and flows towards the counter electrode (CE). The presence of platinum catalyst allows the electron to interact with the tri-iodide ion in the electrolyte system and reduces it to the iodide ion. The iodide ion transfers electron to the sensitizer resulting to the regeneration of the sensitizer in its ground state completing the circuit.

The power conversion efficiency (%PCE) (Table 1 ) shows better performance compared to the negative control but still gave low efficiency compared to the positive control (2.33%) and the highest reported efficiency (0.87%) of CND-SSC from literature [45]. The low efficiency can be attributed to fluorescence properties of CNDs [44], which serves as a charge recombination pathway, affecting the utilization of photo-excited electron for energy generation. Comparing the two CNDs the CNC-CNDs served as better sensitizer than CNDs from RC having efficiency at 0.0176%.

The inefficient adsorption of the carbon nanodots into the TiO2 surface could contribute to the low yield since aggregated CND particles prevent effective adsorption into the TiO2. Although the observed alcohol and carbonyl functional groups in CNDs facilitate binding to TiO2 surface through bidentate-bridging and H-bonding [10,29] these functional groups can also cause inter-molecular hydrogen bonding between CNDs that eventually cause clustering/aggregation. Cluster formation can lead to charge recombination lowering the efficiency of the solar cell. The result is consistent with the literature that overall performance of the DSSC can be based on the effective adsorption of CNDs into the TiO2. Improvement in efficiency was usually done by doping the CNDs with heteroatom [45–48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical impedance measurement of carbon nanodots sensitized solar cell
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) describes the electron transfer processes happening in the cell. The obtained Nyquist plot (Fig. 8 ) has one characteristic semi-circle plot similar to the impedance results of Zhang et al., [43]. The dominant semi-circle reflects the charge transfer at the counter electrode and the charge transfer between the TiO2 layer and electrolyte [49].

The diameter of the semi-circle plot is related to the total impedance of the solar cell [50] and correlated to the conversion efficiency, where a smaller semicircle diameter gives a lower overall device resistance thus exhibiting higher solar efficiency (Table 1). Generally, the diameter of each Nyquist plot is larger compared to that of Zhang et al. [43]. The Nyquist plots were interpreted using the equivalent circuit model (Fig. 8 inset), which gives the series resistance (Rs) and charge-transfer resistance (Rct). These resistances describe the electron transfer processes occurring in the interfaces/components of the solar cell particularly between the FTO conducting glass and the TiO2-CND film [49]. Results show that the Rs and Rct data is consistent with the obtained energy conversion efficiencies where lower resistances afforded higher device efficiency. The CND sensitizer from CNC gave lower resistances indicating a more effective adsorbance into the TiO2 surface and into the conducting glass allowing for a better photo-electron injection compared to the CNDs from RC. There is also a correlation of inherent luminescence of CNDs that relates to the electron recombination of the photo-injected electron hindering the electron transfer towards other parts of the solar cell to complete the cycle and produce electricity.

Carbon nanodots (CNDs) from two different forms of cellulose precursors were successfully prepared using hydrothermal carbonization and the ease of CND formation was shown to be influenced by the size and morphology of the cellulose precursor. The nanometer-sized crystalline CNCs form higher yield and aggregated CNDs compared to the micrometer-sized amorphous raw cellulose (RC). FT-IR and TOF-SIMs confirmed the formation of hydrophilic functionalities like hydroxyl, carbonyl, and oxides on the CNDs surface after carbonization indicating the presence of good anchoring groups for effective adherence to surfaces such as the TiO2 in dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC). Moreover, optical characterization of the photoluminescent CNDs using UV–Vis and Fluorescence Spectroscopy supported the structural and functional group analysis of the CNDs. Fabrication of a TiO2-based solar cell showed the potential of CND as sensitizer albeit in low yield, which may pave the way for the utilization of this abundant but environmentally polluting green algae.
None.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to Dr. Erwin Enriquez and Anna San Esteban for the solar measurements. This work was supported by research grants from the Department of Science and Technology (DOST) of the Republic of the Philippines through the DOST-PCIEERD ADMATEL-EPDC Grant and the DOST-SEI-ASTHRDP scholarship grant for J. C. Sta. Ana.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation and application of fluorescent carbon dots

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: DSSC: Dye Sensitized Solar Cells. https://www.gamry.com/(accessed 5 11 2017).
